coredata.dc:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots for hypochlorous acid detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Cheng
Liu, Meilin
Li, Taotao
Liu, Shengjun
Chen, Qian
Zhang, Jian
Zhang, Kui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using sodium citrate (SC), triethylenetetramine (TETA) and rose bengal (RB) as raw materials. Under the hydrothermal treatment, the carboxyl group in SC can crosslink with the amino group in TETA to form polymer-like intermediate by amide bond network. The RB molecule, a fluorescein derivative, lost halogen atoms under hydrothermal conditions and integrated with the skeleton structure of the network intermediate. Further dehydration and carbonization resulted in the dual-emission fluorescent CDs. Excited by ultraviolet (UV) light, the CDs exhibited blue and green luminescence centered at 440 nm and 525 nm, respectively. Due to the emission centers of these two colors showed different sensitivity to hypochlorous acid (HClO), we have designed a fluorescence assay to achieve quantitative detection of HClO. Upon addition of different concentrations of HClO to CDs solution in phosphate buffer (pH = 6), the blue emission decreased gradually while the green emission showed only a minimal decrease. By calculating the emission intensity ratio, a good linear relationship was achieved in the range of 0–17 μM, and the limit of detection was as low as 0.75 μM. The results reported here demonstrated a promising way to design organic dye modified CDs for sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rose bengal
Dual-emission
Hydrothermal synthesis
Hypochlorous acid

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Emission spectra of as-synthesized CDs under different excitation wavelength. Inset shows the photograph under 300 nm and 350 nm excitation, respectively. (b) The excitation spectra (emission at 440 nm and 525 nm) of as-synthesized CDs. (c) The pH dependent emission behavior of the dual-emission CDs. (d) Fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 440 nm and 525 nm during continuous UV excitation.

(a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis mechanism for the dual-emission CDs. (b) TEM image of as-synthesized CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of the dual-emission CDs. (d) The XPS full survey of as-synthesized CDs, and the high-resolution spectra of C 1s (e), N 1s (f), and O 1s (g).

(a) Room-temperature emission spectra (excitation at 330 nm) of the CDs dispersion in the presence and absence of HClO (10 μM). (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs dispersion in the presence and absence of HClO (10 μM). (c) Fluorescence quenching of dual-emission CDs by HClO (10 μM) at different pH value. (d) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity change (at 440 nm) of CDs with 10 μM of HClO.

(a) Emission spectra of dual-emission CDs in phosphate buffer (pH = 6) with different amount of HClO under 330 nm excitation. (b) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I525) and concentration of HClO.

The selectivity of dual-emission fluorescent CDs toward various potential interfering substances. The concentration of the interfering substance is 20 μM. Inset shows the photograph under UV excitation.
Recovery test of proposed method for HClO in tap water.

head.ce:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots for hypochlorous acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maxiang Road, Ma'anshan, Anhui, 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maxiang Road, Ma'anshan, Anhui, 243032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using sodium citrate (SC), triethylenetetramine (TETA) and rose bengal (RB) as raw materials. Under the hydrothermal treatment, the carboxyl group in SC can crosslink with the amino group in TETA to form polymer-like intermediate by amide bond network. The RB molecule, a fluorescein derivative, lost halogen atoms under hydrothermal conditions and integrated with the skeleton structure of the network intermediate. Further dehydration and carbonization resulted in the dual-emission fluorescent CDs. Excited by ultraviolet (UV) light, the CDs exhibited blue and green luminescence centered at 440 nm and 525 nm, respectively. Due to the emission centers of these two colors showed different sensitivity to hypochlorous acid (HClO), we have designed a fluorescence assay to achieve quantitative detection of HClO. Upon addition of different concentrations of HClO to CDs solution in phosphate buffer (pH = 6), the blue emission decreased gradually while the green emission showed only a minimal decrease. By calculating the emission intensity ratio, a good linear relationship was achieved in the range of 0–17 μM, and the limit of detection was as low as 0.75 μM. The results reported here demonstrated a promising way to design organic dye modified CDs for sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Disinfection is an important segment of the urban water treatment process [1]. Through the strong oxidation effect on pathogenic microorganisms, the disinfectant inhibits the spread of diseases, including cholera, typhoid fever, and so on [2]. Particularly, chlorine disinfectant is a commonly used method in water disinfection technology because of its strong sterilizing ability, convenient use and low cost [3]. However, excessive exposure of human skin to hypochlorous acid (HClO) can cause a series of symptoms such as allergies [4]. At the same time, the residual of HClO can also react with organic substances in the water to form disinfection by-products, which are carcinogenic and mutagenic [5,6]. Therefore, it is of great practical significance to develop a rapid and sensitive method to detect HClO in water.

In recent years, many simple and precise methods have been developed for analyzing of HClO, including colorimetric method [7,8], electrochemical sensor [9,10] and fluorescence assay [11–15]. Among these methods, fluorescence method using organic dye probe has received extensive attention due to its high sensitivity and selectivity. Hypochlorite (ClO−) and its protonated form (HClO) showed strong oxidation, which can react with p-methoxyphenol to benzoquinone [16]. The specific reaction prohibited corresponding photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process, while the product is highly luminescent. Besides, the oximes reacted with hypochlorite rapidly under mild conditions through deoximation reaction, which provided a useful way to design a fluorescent probe for hypochlorite [17]. In recent years, fluorescent nanomaterials have been widely developed as an effective luminophore in HClO analysis [18,19]. Compared with organic dye probe, the fluorescent nanomaterials for HClO sensing still have the drawbacks including low solubility and high cost. Therefore, exploring of novel luminescent nanomaterials for HClO detection still remains tremendous research interest.

Carbon dots (CDs), as the zero-dimensional nanomaterials emerged in recent years, have important applications in many fields, including display devices [20,21], bioimaging [22–24], and chemical sensors [25–27]. A wide variety of chemical sensors based on CDs have been developed to achieve the detection of metal ions [28,29], environmental pollutant [30,31] or biomolecules [32–34]. On the one hand, CDs exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield and excellent water solubility. Only a small amount of sample is required to complete the analysis and measurement during the detection process. On the other hand, the synthesis method for CDs is simple and convenient, which lays a foundation for promoting its practical applications. Ratiometric fluorescent probe consisting of dual-emission bands exhibited higher sensitivity than single-emission [12]. However, it is worth noting that most of the reported CDs generate only one emission band under single excitation, which limits their application in the field of highly sensitive analytical probes. In this work, we conjugated rose bengal (RB) with CDs via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment. The novel type of CDs generated blue and green dual-emission color at a single wavelength excitation. Moreover, these two emission bands exhibit different sensitiveness to HClO. Based on these findings, we designed a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of HClO. The blue fluorescence of CDs was quenched by HClO and the green fluorescence was used as reference signal. The proposed method was successfully applied to HClO detection in tap water samples.

Triethylenetetramine (TETA, 70%), sodium hypochlorite (the concentration was determined by iodometric titration), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4) and sodium dihydngen phoshate (NaH2PO4) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. Rose bengal (RB) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium citrate (SC), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), potassium chlorate (KClO3), potassium bromate (KBrO3) and other inorganic salts were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents and materials are used directly without further purification.

The preparation of dual-emission CDs employed one-pot hydrothermal synthesis method. In brief, 10 mg of RB and 0.5 g of SC were added to 15 mL of ultrapure water to form homogeneous solution. Then, 1 mL of TETA was added under vigorous stirring. Finally, the above solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room-temperature, the dual-emission CDs were obtained. The prepared CDs were purified by dialysis using a dialysis bag (1000, molecular weight cutoff) for 24 h to remove unreacted RB and small molecule byproduct. The final products were stored at 4 °C refrigerator for further use.

In order to evaluate the determination of hypochlorous acid in solutions by dual-emission CDs, different amount of sodium hypochlorite were added to the phosphate buffer solution (pH = 6). In brief, a series of solutions containing different amounts of sodium hypochlorite were mixed with 10 μL of CDs solution (1 mg/mL), and the solutions were diluted to 3 mL with phosphate buffer in 1 cm quartz cuvette. After incubation for 5 min at room-temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The transmission electron microscope (JEOL, JEM-2100) was used to observe the morphology of CDs. The Fourier transfer infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained by Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrometer using KBr pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were performed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7100) equipped with a R928 photomultiplier tube (PMT). All photographs were taken with a Canon 600D digital camera.

First of all, the dual-emission properties of as-synthesized CDs were systematically investigated. Under excitation of UV light, the CDs (purified CDs in ultrapure water) exhibited distinct dual emission bands: a blue emission at 440 nm and a green emission at 525 nm (Fig. 1 a). It should be noted that the peak positions of the two emissions have not changed with tuning the excitation wavelength in the range of 300–350 nm. However, the relative intensity of dual emissions has changed dramatically with the excitation wavelength. By calculating the ratio of emission intensity (I440/I525), the relative intensity is varied from 0.0864 (300 nm excitation) to 3.2661 (350 nm excitation) (Table S1). Therefore, the aqueous solution of CDs showed green and blue luminescence under 300 and 350 nm excitation, respectively (inset of Fig. 1a). The reason for excitation-dependent phenomenon can be explained from the excitation spectra. As shown in Fig. 1b, the optimal excitation for blue emission is located at 360 nm while the optimum for green emission is located at 325 nm. Therefore, the emission intensity ratio of the two peaks can be adjusted by changing the excitation wavelength. The pH value of dispersion is another key factor to influence the emission intensity ratio of dual-emission (Fig. 1c). Upon 330 nm excitation in the weak acidic condition, the emission intensity at 440 nm is much higher than that at 525 nm. However, the emission at 525 nm is more effective in alkaline media. The above results imply that the blue and green luminescence should be derived from two different emission centers in synthesized CDs.

In addition, the two emissions exhibited high photostability. When the CDs dispersion was continuously irradiated by UV light for 1 h, negligible decline of fluorescence intensities was observed (Fig. 1d). Thanks to the excellent dispersibility, the CDs dispersion was employed as fluorescent ink to write letters on filter paper (Fig. S1), which demonstrated the potential application as a luminescent pigment.

In our experiment, TETA, SC and RB, were utilized as starting materials to synthesize CDs (Fig. 2 a). Among them, the TETA molecule contains a plurality of amino and imino groups, while the SC is rich in carboxyl groups. Under hydrothermal conditions, dehydration and condensation occurs between the amino group and the carboxyl group to form a polymer-like intermediate, followed by carbonization process. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 2b revealed that the synthesized CDs are quasi-spherical in shape with very small size. The size distribution of CDs from TEM image indicated an average diameter of 2.8 nm (Fig. S2).

The FT-IR spectrum of dual-emission CDs showed obvious absorption peaks from 3700 to 3100 cm−1, which could be attributed to the vibration of O–H and N–H bond (Fig. 2c). Peaks centered around 2927 and 2854 cm−1 illustrated the C–H stretching in CDs. Characteristic peaks of amide structure could be detected at 1600 cm−1 (CO) and 1312 cm−1 (C–N). The elements and binding mode of the designed CDs were investigated in detail. As shown in the full survey XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. 2d), three main elements of C, N and O on the surface of CDs are identified. The high resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) revealed four different carbon bond states: C–C/CC (284.6 eV), C–N (285.4 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and –COOH (287.9 eV) [35]. Peak fitting of N 1s (Fig. 2f) demonstrated three bonding states: C–NC (399.2 eV), N-(C)3 (400.1 eV) and N–H (401.3 eV) [36]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) showed two different types of oxygen: CO (530.9 eV) and C–O (532.1 eV) [37]. All the results confirmed that the CDs were composed of carbonized amide network and various surface functional groups.

Previous reports have shown that the formed amide bond network and functional groups (CO, CN, NH2) have a significant impact on the production of blue fluorescence [38,39]. These electron-rich moieties crosslinked and generated new energy levels, leading to radiative transitions. As a control experiment, we synthesized CDs only by TETA and SC under identical condition (denoted as B-CDs, Fig. S3). Strong blue emission at 440 nm was also obtained from the B-CDs, which confirmed that the amide bond between TETA and SC is responsible for the blue luminescence.

RB molecule is a fluorescein derivative, which contains four iodine atoms (Fig. S4). These iodine atoms eliminated from RB easily under hydrothermal reaction due to the unstable C–I bond [40]. On the other hand, the carboxyl group of RB connected with TETA via chemical coupling. Thus, the green emission was introduced to the CDs. The XPS experiment carried out the low relative content of iodine in as-synthesized CDs (Table S2). This halide loss process can also be explained by fluorescence spectral changes. The fluorescence emission of RB located at 566 nm, while that of fluorescein located at 514 nm [41]. In designed CDs, the green emission located at 525 nm, the blue-shift of emission fully confirmed the iodine loss under hydrothermal treatment. To sum up, the blue emission originated from polyamide network and the green emission center originated from halide-lost RB modifier.

Interestingly, the two emission peaks of the as-synthesized CDs showed different sensitivity to HClO in solution. In order to excite the two emission bands synchronously, the excitation wavelength of 330 nm was chosen for experiments. As shown in Fig. 3 a, we found that the blue emission at 440 nm was significantly quenched in the pH = 6 phosphate buffer, and its fluorescence intensity decreased by 30.5% when 10 μM of HClO was added to the CDs dispersion. However, the green emission at 525 nm exhibited only 11.1% decline. The quenching efficiency of blue emission was ~3-fold higher than green emission. In order to verify the reason for the change in the blue fluorescence intensity by HClO, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs before and after the incubation with HClO were performed (Fig. 3b). The absorption peak at 350 nm is derived from the n→π* transition of CDs skeleton, and the absorption in the range of 480–510 nm is mainly attributed to the conjugated structure of RB. When HClO was added to the CDs dispersion, the UV–vis absorption spectra showed negligible change, indicating that the core structure of CDs was not destroyed. Recently, it has been found that there is an essential relationship between the luminescence of CDs and their surface states [42,43]. Accordingly, the addition of HClO will destroy the passivation layer on the surface of CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching of blue emission.

Since the pH value of the reaction system has a significant impact on the oxidation property of HClO, we investigated the changes of fluorescence intensity ratio of dual-emission CDs before and after the addition of HClO under different pH conditions. It can be seen from Fig. 3c that HClO can change the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I440/I525) to the maximum extent in a weakly acidic environment with pH value at 6. HClO shows strong oxidizing in a weakly acidic environment, which further confirms that the fluorescence quenching of CDs is due to the oxidation effect by HClO. Finally, we monitored the time-dependent curve of the luminescence intensity of CDs at 440 nm in response to HClO (Fig. 3d). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity no longer changed after the addition of HClO for 5 min, which confirmed that the fluorescence response of CDs to HClO can construct a chemical sensor to realize sensitive and rapid detection of HClO.

In order to understand the slight decline of green emission, we recorded a broader excitation spectrum for 525 nm and the results showed that the emission light at 440 nm can also excite the green emission at 525 nm (Fig. S5a). Therefore, partial fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from blue band to green band was existed in the dual-emission system. We mixed fluorescein with the single-emission B-CDs dispersion and recorded the fluorescence spectra (Fig. S5b). Due to the overlap of blue emission and fluorescein excitation, the peak width and intensity of blue emission decreased with addition of fluorescein. Furthermore, the CDs with small size of ~2.8 nm satisfied the distance requirement of FRET (<10 nm). Introducing of HClO quenched the blue emission and the FRET process was destroyed, which resulted to the slight decline of green emission.

Under optimal conditions discussed above (pH value and reaction time), the quantitative determination of HClO in solution was successfully achieved. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the concentration of HClO in the dual-emission platform increased gradually, the blue luminescence at 440 nm decreased significantly in the concentration range of 0–17 μM, while the green luminescence at 525 nm showed only slight decline. By calculating the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I525), the ratio of two emissions showed a good linear relationship (Y = 1.983–0.0516X, R2 = 0.992) with the addition concentration of HClO (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.75 μM with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Based on the sensitivity of the blue emission to HClO and the green emission as internal standard, the sensing performance of the simple and effective method is better than the reported nanomaterial-based fluorescence sensors (Table S3). Additionally, the dual-emission probe opens a way to fabricate portable devices for HClO sensing [44].

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed dual-emission fluorescence assay, inorganic ions and other oxidants were examined on the fluorescence of CDs, including MnO4 −, BrO3 −, ClO3 −, NO3 −, SO4 2−, NO2 −, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, F−, Cl−, and Br− (Fig. 5 ). The comparison was established based on the relative strength change of (F0–F)/F0, where F0 represented the fluorescence intensity ratio of I440/I525 in the absence of interference ions, F represented the fluorescence intensity ratio in the presence of interference ions. Interestingly, the oxidants of MnO4 −, BrO3 − and ClO3 − did not cause obvious relative intensity change, which indicated that the dual-emission fluorescent CDs discriminate the hypochlorite effectively. Moreover, the common interference ions in drinking water or tap water displayed negligible impact on the fluorescence of CDs, suggesting the excellent selectivity for HClO response.

The developed sensing platform was successfully used for HClO detection in tap water. Boiled tap water with different amount of sodium hypochlorite was employed as spiked samples and the residue was detected by proposed method. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery of the standard addition method is 104.4% and 100.5% for 5 and 10 μM of hypochlorite, respectively, indicating the accurate and reliable results for dual-emission CDs based sensing system. Moreover, the concentrations of spiked samples were also detected by DPD (N, N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine) colorimetric methods [45], the results showed fine consistency compared with the fluorescence method. The demonstration illustrated the potential applications for fast and on-site water quality monitoring using fluorescent CDs.

In conclusion, we successfully integrated two kinds of emission centers to obtain dual-emission fluorescent CDs through one-pot hydrothermal method. Under single wavelength UV light excitation, the as-synthesized CDs generated blue and green emission bands simultaneously. The blue fluorescence originated from the amide group and the green fluorescence was attributed to the halide-lost RB modifier, respectively. The two emission centers exhibited distinct sensitivity to the oxidant of hypochlorous acid, and a ratiometric fluorescence assay for HClO detection was established accordingly. The ratiometric emission intensity linearly varied with the concentration of HClO, accompanying the LOD of 0.75 μM. Due to the successful demonstration of dual-emission CDs, we confirm that the organic dye modified nanomaterials can be further designed and applied in the field of chemical sensors.

Cheng Zhang: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Meilin Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Taotao Li: Visualization, Resources. Shengjun Liu: Formal analysis, Resources. Qian Chen: Validation, Resources. Jian Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Kui Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675158 and 21976002), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1908085MB41).
